statement.en: USING LOCAL SPECTRAL LIBRARIES TO CREATE PREDICTION MODELS FOR SOIL PHYSICAL PROPERTY ASSESSMENT IN WASHINGTON STATE
statement.summary: Soil compaction is a major and extensive problem within agriculture, and we do not have standardized way to measure compaction or its effects. We can use tools such as penetrometers for rapid assessment of soil compaction; however, these measurements require data on soil organic carbon, clay content, and water content. This study was conducted to set the groundwork for visible and near infrared (VisNIR) as well as mid-infrared (MIR) spectrometers to measure multiple soil properties with one measurement. To evaluate MIR spectroscopy, I evaluated the impact of local vs. statewide spectral libraries for predicting organic carbon and clay content using lab analyzed soils from Washington State University and a larger statewide dataset derived from the Kellogg Soil Survey Laboratory. Additionall

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The work is a 2026-dated empirical study (future publication or preprint status unclear) presenting methodology for soil property prediction using spectroscopy. The core claims—that local spectral libraries outperform or complement statewide libraries for VisNIR/MIR prediction of soil properties—are contingent on complete results (abstract cuts off mid-sentence). The approach is sound and reproducible, but validation status is incomplete and results-dependent.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 光が土の記憶を読み取り、近い知識が遠い知識より早く答えに到達する。
